coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in fluorescent probes for ATP imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chen
Wu, Guanzhao
coredata.dc:description: Adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) is a critical biological molecule that functions as the primary energy currency within cells. ATP synthesis occurs in the mitochondria, and variations in its concentration can significantly influence mitochondrial and cellular performance. Prior studies have established a link between ATP levels and a variety of diseases, such as cancer, neurodegenerative conditions, ischemia, and hypoglycemia. Consequently, researchers have developed many fluorescent probes for ATP detection, recognizing the importance of monitoring intracellular ATP levels to understand cellular processes. These probes have been effectively utilized for visualizing ATP in living cells and biological samples. In this comprehensive review, we categorize fluorescent sensors developed in the last five years for ATP detection. We base our classification on fluorophores, structure, multi-response channels, and application. We also evaluate the challenges and potential for advancing new generations of fluorescence imaging probes for monitoring ATP in living cells. We hope this summary motivates researchers to design innovative and effective probes tailored to ATP sensing. We foresee imminent progress in the development of highly sophisticated ATP probes.

Nanoprobes
Small-molecule probes

head.ce:title: Recent advances in fluorescent probes for ATP imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Central Laboratory and Mitochondrial Medicine Laboratory, Qilu Hospital (Qingdao), Cheeloo College of Medicine, Shandong University, Qingdao, 266035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Central Laboratory and Mitochondrial Medicine Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Central Laboratory and Mitochondrial Medicine Laboratory, Qilu Hospital (Qingdao), Cheeloo College of Medicine, Shandong University, Qingdao, 266035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) is a critical biological molecule that functions as the primary energy currency within cells. ATP synthesis occurs in the mitochondria, and variations in its concentration can significantly influence mitochondrial and cellular performance. Prior studies have established a link between ATP levels and a variety of diseases, such as cancer, neurodegenerative conditions, ischemia, and hypoglycemia. Consequently, researchers have developed many fluorescent probes for ATP detection, recognizing the importance of monitoring intracellular ATP levels to understand cellular processes. These probes have been effectively utilized for visualizing ATP in living cells and biological samples. In this comprehensive review, we categorize fluorescent sensors developed in the last five years for ATP detection. We base our classification on fluorophores, structure, multi-response channels, and application. We also evaluate the challenges and potential for advancing new generations of fluorescence imaging probes for monitoring ATP in living cells. We hope this summary motivates researchers to design innovative and effective probes tailored to ATP sensing. We foresee imminent progress in the development of highly sophisticated ATP probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP), a pivotal biological molecule synthesized in mitochondria, plays a central role in cellular functions. ATP comprises adenine, ribose, and three phosphate groups (Fig. 1 ) [1]. As an energy carrier, the hydrolysis of ATP to ADP releases inorganic phosphate, providing free energy for various biological processes such as biosynthesis, chromosome maintenance, macromolecular transport, and cell motility [1–3]. Additionally, ATP is integral to the synthesis of DNA, RNA, and NAD(P) [4] and serves as a signaling molecule for cell-cell communication in the nervous system [5,6] and immune regulation [7]. Thus, ATP is essential for maintaining a healthy state.

In animal cells, ATP was primarily produced through glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) [8]. It has been reported that the ATP concentration varies significantly, ranging from 1 to 10 mM in living cells and mitochondria [9–12]. Comparatively, neurons have the lowest ATP concentrations of ∼3 mM [13]. However, the concentration of intracellular ATP always fluctuates, responding to nutrients, hormones, cytotoxic agents, and hypoxia [14,15], even depending on states [16]. The abnormal ATP expression in humans also plays a role as a clinical diagnostic indicator, such as deficit production in the spinal cord and neuropathic pain [17–19], a significant increase in cancer cells [12,20–22], possibly ascribed into the energy requirement inactivated or overactivated by cells [19]. However, current detection methods face limitations such as poor selectivity, short emission wavelengths, limited detection ranges, and lack of cell-targeting specificity [23–25]. Therefore, developing a chemical tool for real-time monitoring of ATP levels in vitro and in vivo is highly desirable.

Fluorescence imaging has emerged as a powerful technology for real-time, noninvasive detection of biomarkers in cellular and animal research. Recently developed fluorescent probes enable precise identification and visualization for ATP. These probes overcome cytotoxicity challenges and exhibit remarkable selectivity, distinguishing ATP from closely related analogs as well as various common interfering ions, amino acids, and other biomolecules. Additionally, an effective ATP fluorescent probe requires stability, rapid response, minimal photodamage, and a specific binding site with ATP. Fluorescence imaging has become indispensable for real-time ATP visualization. In this review, we provide a summary and comparison of advances in fluorescence probes for ATP detection from the last five years. We categorize them based on fluorophores, structure, multi-response channels, and application (Fig. 1). We also discuss the challenges and opportunities for developing future generations of fluorescence imaging probes for monitoring ATP in living cells. We hope this review accelerates the development and application of fluorescent probes tailored for visualizing ATP in living cells, offering significant potential for diagnosing and treating various human diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent ATP probes based on fluorophores

ce:section.ce:para: Rhodamine-based fluorescence probes are widely available commercially. Huo et al. [26] designed and synthesized a novel rhodamine-based probe 1 for ATP detection (Fig. 2 a). The design of probe 1 incorporates rhodamine as an ATP-responsive fluorophore, diethylenetriamine as an ATP-binding linker, and a naphthalimide group as a tracking fluorophore. In the absence of ATP, probes emit green fluorescence from the naphthalimide group at approximately 530 nm. However, upon ATP addition, they exhibit near-infrared (NIR) emission by opening the non-fluorescent spirocyclic form at around 600 nm. Remarkably, probe 1 can detect changes in ATP levels when mitochondria are exposed to exogenous H2O2 and H2S, offering potential insights into diseases associated with ATP imbalance (Fig. 2b).

Similarly, Juyoung's lab developed three rhodamine-based probes (Fig. 3 acd), which all employ thiourea moiety and rhodamine to recognize and image ATP, respectively. Furthermore, probe 2 can be tracked at ∼530 nm without any change upon the addition of ATP due to the introduction of the naphthalimide group [27]. Probe 3 incorporated a morpholine group to facilitate lysosomal targeting, while probe 4 is designed to target mitochondrial ATP, owing to its lipophilicity and charge [28]. Specifically, those probes showed higher selectivity, effectively discriminating against various other anionic species and metal cations (Fig. 3bef).

Carbon dots (CDs), a group of quasi-spherical nanoparticles belonging to quantum dots, offer benefits such as straightforward synthesis, smaller size (<10 nm), exceptional fluorescence properties, good water solubility, and favorable biocompatibility. These qualities make CDs increasingly attractive in the fields of sensing and biological imaging [29–35].

Mina et al. designed a novel CDs-Quercetin-Fluoride ions system (CD-QCT-F-) probe 5 (Fig. 4 a) [36], a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for detecting Zn2+ and ATP. The addition of Zn2+ to the CD-QCT-F- system leads to the formation of CD-QCT-F--Zn2+, which significantly quenches the red fluorescence of CDs and increases the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 480 nm and 610 nm (I480/I610). By introducing a specific amount of F−, potential interference from Al3+ on Zn2+ can be mitigated. ATP's stronger affinity for Zn2+ compared to quercetin (QCT) causes a gradual decrease in the I480/I610 value with sequential ATP addition. Fig. 3b illustrates the fluorescence changes at I480 and I610 of probe 5 in response to varying concentrations of Zn2+ and ATP.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) to fluorescent dyes is a promising technique for bioanalysis in living cells. Chengzhi et al. [37] combined carbon dots (CDs) as FRET donors with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) as FRET acceptors. This complex, named probe 6 (Fig. 5 a), was further functionalized with triphenylphosphine (TPP) for mitochondrial targeting. Upon ATP introduction, probe 6 aggregates, causing the solution to become turbid, a notable increase in hydrodynamic size (Fig. 5b), and an enhancement in FRET efficiency from 15.4 % to 53.2 % as calculated by the decrease in fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 5c). Therefore, probe 6 effectively monitors ATP level fluctuations within mitochondria (Fig. 5d).

Changjun and his colleagues synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon dot, known as probe 7 (Fig. 6 a) [38], using hydrothermal synthesis with polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA). When ATP was added, an aggregation-induced enhancement effect (AIE) was observed in probe 7, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity at 460 nm (Fig. 6b). Remarkably, it's recorded that probe 7 showed quick response to the presence of ATP, exhibiting fluorescence in 3 min (Fig. 6c).

Thioflavin T (ThT) is a molecular dye with a low fluorescence background, good chemical stability, and water solubility [39]. When ThT interacts with nucleic acids, it orients itself vertically, leading to increased fluorescence emission [40].

Prabhat and colleagues developed a supramolecular assembly, known as probe 8 [41], by forming H-aggregates of ThT on the surface of polyanionic sulphated-β-cyclodextrin (SCD) (Fig. 7 a). The probe achieves ratiometric detection through modulation of the monomer-aggregate equilibrium in the ThT-SCD system: the fluorescence is turned off upon interaction with Zn2+ ions (disassembly), and subsequently turned on upon interaction with ATP (re-assembly). Notably, probe 8 exhibited a strong response to ATP in human serum samples (Fig. 7b).

Haifei and his team developed an ATP aptamer-ThT G-quadruplex complex known as probe 9 [42]. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, in the absence of ATP, the ThT dye binds to the G-rich ATP aptamer, forming an ATP aptamer-ThT G-quadruplex complex, which leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity. However, the fluorescence intensity decreases upon the addition of ATP because ATP has a high affinity for the G-rich ATP aptamer and can displace ThT, forming an ATP aptamer-ATP complex. Notably, probe 9 introduces a new fluorescent dye, butyl rhodamine B (BRB), to create a calibration model, effectively mitigating the impact of scatterers and background absorbers in cell suspensions (Fig. 8b).

Probe 10 [43] is an aptamer-based, label-free sensing platform consisting of five functional domains, as depicted in Fig. 9 a. When ATP is added, it binds to the ATP-binding aptamer in probe 10, causing a structural transformation. The released binding domain AS1 hybridizes with AS2, triggering KF polymerase-dependent strand extension by a C-rich fragment and recycling. Then, Nt.BbvCI-triggered excision at the black region generates numerous G-quadruplex sequences that associate with ThT, resulting in substantially amplified fluorescence. This method achieves high sensitivity in ATP measurements, down to 2.2 nM in the range of 5–200 nM without using any labels (Fig. 9b).

Two carbocyanine dyes, Cy3 and Cy5, form a common dye pair used to construct a FRET system, functioning as donor and acceptor, respectively [44]. By minimizing the distance between Cy3 and Cy5, distinct ratiometric signals can be generated [45].

Probe 11 is prepared through a “one-pot” DNA hybridization of six DNA strands, with A6 serving as an ATP aptamer sequence and featuring Cy3 and Cy5 at the two ends (Fig. 10 a) [46]. Meanwhile, probe 12 is developed through the sequential hybridization of two hairpin DNA strands (HS1-Cy3 and HS2-Cy5), which contain split ATP aptamer fragments labeled with Cy3 and Cy5, respectively (Fig. 10b) [47]. In Ca2+-treated cells, both probes displayed lower Cy3 fluorescence and stronger Cy5 fluorescence, producing a higher FRET ratiometric signal. These results suggest that both probes effectively detect ATP, responding to physiologically relevant changes.

Two-photon fluorescence imaging, which offers reduced background interference and greater penetration depth, has significant application value [48,49]. YongXiang et al. introduced a novel two-photon fluorescent assembly, probe 13 [50], composed of a two-photon dye (2-naphthalenecarboxylic acid)-doped silica nanoparticle (NP) core (TP-SiNP), a hairpin primer with carboxyfluorescein (FAM), and an ATP aptamer (Fig. 10a). As depicted in Scheme 11b, the strong affinity between the ATP aptamer and environmental ATP causes the primer strand to form a hairpin structure, activating FRET through FAM for ATP measurement (Fig. 11 b). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity ratio showed a correlation within the range of 0 mM–5.0 mM ATP concentration, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.08 nM (R2 = 0.9792) (Fig. 11c). It is worth noting that probe 13 demonstrated satisfactory ratiometric two-photon fluorescence imaging of endogenous ATP in living MCF-7 cells (Fig. 11d) and a rat liver tissue slice (with a penetration depth of 190 nm) (Fig. 11e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent ATP probes based on the structure

ce:section.ce:para: Many molecules in the biological system have similar structures to ATP, such as adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and adenosine diphosphate (ADP). The sensing mechanisms of the probe should be designed precisely based on ATP's composition, including adenine base, ribose, and especially unique triphosphate moiety.

Common non-covalent bonds between host and target molecules include hydrogen bonds, electrostatic attraction, hydrophilic and hydrophobic interactions, and π-π stacking. Additionally, chemical bonds form between aryl boronic acid and vicinal diols in the ribose.

Dokyoung et al. reported a molecule, probe 14 (Fig. 12 a) [51], consisting of tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and mono-triamine (TA), which forms an aggregated type in aqueous media. TA targets triphosphate in ATP through electrostatic attraction, with π-π stacking between benzene rings from TPE and the adenine portion of ATP, leading to better formation of ATP complex than ADP and AMP. Remarkably, probe 14 exhibits high selectivity toward ATP (Fig. 12b), reliable sensitivity with a LOD of around 0.275 ppb, and a rapid response time of 10 s (Fig. 12c).

Similarly, probe 15, developed by Zhilin's lab (Fig. 13 a) [52], features a novel D-π-A structure based on TPE and dual positive charge recognition sites. These sites enable probe 15 to utilize π-π stacking interactions and electrostatic absorption for ATP recognition. Additionally, probe 15 demonstrated excellent properties with a LOD of 3.62 μM.

Jong Seung Kim and his team introduced a novel nanostructured chemosensing ensemble, probe 16 (Fig. 13b) [53], created by self-assembling uranine dye (UD) and an amphiphilic pyridinium salt, PyNp-C13. Due to photo-induced electron transfer (PET) quenching from the pyridinium group of PyNp-C13 to UD, the ensemble exhibited very weak fluorescence. However, competition between ATP and PyNp-C13, through both electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking, displaced UD from the host and resulted in a significant increase in fluorescence. This indicator displacement assay (IDA) method achieves an ultra-low detection limit of 6.8 nM.

Probe 17, which had a pincer-like benzene structure stacking for fluorescent sensing of ATP, was designed by Juyoung Yoon's group. With phosphate-containing anions, probe 17 displays a large fluorescent quenching effect in its excimer emission. While in the presence of adenine base, it showed fluorescent enhancement in its monomeric emission by forming unique sandwich stacking of pyrene-adenine-pyrene, to distinguish from other phosphate-containing anions, like CTP, PPT, GTP, and UTP (Fig. 13c) [54]. Meanwhile, due to the anionic charges and the difference in the adenine ring stacking between the two pyrene moieties, ADP and AMP display a very limited fluorescent quenching effect (Fig. 13d).

Aptamers are artificial single-stranded DNA or RNA molecules that hold significant promise for bioanalytical applications due to their straightforward synthesis, high specificity, and affinity [55]. For ATP detection, the widely used DNA sequence is 5′-ACCTGGGGGAGTATTGCGGAGGAAGGT-3′ or its modified version [56–58]. This sequence's effectiveness stems from the interaction between ATP and the aptamer, which induces a change in the aptamer's conformation and alters the spatial distance between different fluorophores, thereby triggering the appropriate fluorescence response.

Bhaskar Mohan and his colleagues designed three types of aptamer-based aptasensors using two approaches [56]. Probe 18 (Fig. 14 a) assembled a guanine-rich ATP aptamer sequence and a DNA-binding agent berberine complex. Using “turn-on” approach, the addition of ATP induced strong fluorescence enhancement (green) by forming a G-quadruplex. Comparatively, probe 19 (Fig. 14b) and probe 20 (Fig. 14c) are both FRET-based ATP nanoaptasensors utilizing a quencher and a fluorophore-labeled at the 5′- and 3′-termini of the ATP-binding aptamer sequence, respectively, along with berberine. The difference is that in probe 19, the quencher is AuNRs, and the fluorophore is red quantum dots (RQDs), while in probe 20, they applied AgNPs and CDs. The addition of ATP specifically resulted in probe folding into G-quadruplex, giving rise to an enhancement of fluorescence of berberine while that of RQD and CDs were significantly quenched via FRET.

Chunying et al. employed a turn-on fluorescence approach to construct probe 21 [57], which consists primarily of a DNA template coupled with copper/silver nanoclusters (Cu/Ag NCs). The specific interaction between ATP and its aptamer on the DNA template brings two darkish Cu/Ag NCs close together, initiating fluorescence (Fig. 15 a). Notably, probe 21 exhibits a good linear response to adenosine deaminase (ADA) (Fig. 15b), an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of adenosine to inosine by removing an amino group and is present in all human tissues [59]. Consequently, the probe also serves as an ADA-sensing system.

Probe 22 [58] consists of two elements: an ATP aptamer attached to a 3′ black hole quencher (BHQ) and a 5′ Cy5-labeled reporter strand. In the absence of ATP, the fluorophore of the integrated duplexes is in a quenched state. However, the presence of ATP induces a conformational change in the aptamer, allowing the displacement of the reporter flares (Fig. 16 a). Moreover, probe 22 has been shown to successfully monitor intracellular ATP in human bone osteosarcoma U2OS cells (Fig. 16b) and live cardiomyocytes (Fig. 16c).

Yongyun et al. reported a novel label-free fluorescent “turn-on” biosensor, probe 23 (Fig. 17 a) [60], which includes an ATP-binding aptamer (blue), the conserved bases of Broccoli (green), and a linker (orange). The addition of ATP causes the ATP aptamer to fold, allowing the linker to transmit the signal and enabling the conserved bases of Broccoli to fold into the correct conformation. This allows binding to DFHBI, resulting in a fluorescence intensity that can be used for quantitative analysis of ATP. To achieve ATP monitoring under various conditions, the modified biosensor was cloned into plasmids and subsequently transformed into E. coli and A549 cells. The intracellular biosensor can monitor ATP in living cells, particularly visualizing the dynamics of ATP in living A549 cells (Fig. 17b).

Various amplification strategies have been utilized to improve the limit of detection and achieve ultra-sensitivity. These include cascade amplification, photoinduced amplification, gold nanoparticle-assisted amplification, DNAzyme amplification, silver nanocluster-assisted amplification, cDNA-modified gold nanoparticle amplification, dual-cyclical amplification, controlled-release and cyclic enzymatic amplification, and Exo III-assisted signal amplification.

Both probe 24 [61] and probe 25 [62] use cyclic enzymatic amplification with the assistance of exonuclease III (Exo III). As shown in Fig. 18 a, probe 24 employs an ATP aptamer/silver nanocluster duplex for labeling and sensing, which is quenched when absorbed onto positively charged gold nanorods ((+)AuNRs) in the absence of ATP. The addition of ATP strengthens the electrostatic interaction between the ATP aptamer and (+)AuNRs, further reducing the fluorescence signal of the silver nanoclusters. When Exo III is added, the duplex is hydrolyzed and released, leading to a recovery of the strong fluorescent signal while allowing ATP to be regenerated for the next target cycle.

In probe 25 (Fig. 18b), Rhodamine B-loaded gold nanoclusters modified with short-stranded SH-DNA S1 form the skeleton, with the pore capped by gold nanoparticles coupled with long-stranded SH-DNA S2. The addition of ATP releases primer S3, which has weaker hybridization with the ATP aptamer but hybridizes with the unhybridized part of S2, triggering the release of Rhodamine B with the aid of Exo III. Notably, these two probes can achieve extremely low detection limits for ATP, with 26 pM for probe 24 and 0.88 nM for probe 25.

Luo's group first introduced proximity-enabled bidirectional enzymatic repairing amplification for ATP detection in probe 26 (Fig. 19 ) [63]. In the presence of target ATP, two split aptamer fragments co-recognize ATP to form a stable AP1-ATP-AP2 structure, inducing a conformational switch in the molecular beacon (MB) and initiating intermolecular hybridization. This triggers bidirectional polymerization, extending the MB to form a duplex and releasing the AP1-ATP-AP2 complex. Meanwhile, four lesion sites are removed by cleaving the N-glycosidic bond using uracil DNA glycosylase (UDG) to form an apurinic/apyrimidinic (AP) site. This site is then cleaved by endonuclease IV, generating two new single-stranded DNA fragments, T1 and T2. The liberated T1 and T2 can bind to other MBs, respectively. Therefore, as more AP1-ATP-AP2 structures form, additional rounds of the bidirectional enzymatic repairing amplification reaction can be promoted, resulting in a remarkably enhanced fluorescence signal.

Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are a subclass of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) constructed from metal ions and imidazole linkers [64]. They exhibit remarkable structural diversity, adjustable pore sizes, straightforward synthesis, and versatility for various biological applications, including combating pathogenic bacteria, delivering cancer drugs, and adsorbing environmental pollutants [65,66]. There are primarily two types of ZIFs. ZIF-8 is synthesized through reactions between Zn2+ and 2-methylimidazole (2-MIM), while ZIF-90 results from the combination of Zn2+ and imidazole-2-carboxaldehyde (2-ICA) [66]. Given the stronger coordination between ATP and metal ions compared to that between imidazole linkers and metal ions [67], modified ZIFs have found widespread applications in ATP detection.

Probe 27 (Fig. 20 a) [68] encapsulated Rhodamine B (RhB) as a fluorescent indicator within ZIF-8. Due to its good biocompatibility and high cell permeability, probe 27 effectively illustrated the fluctuation of mitochondrial ATP in living HeLa cells during photodynamic therapy (PDT) (Fig. 20b). Fig. 20c and corresponding box plots (Fig. 20d and e) of HeLa cells treated with Calcein-AM and probe 27 clearly demonstrated that ATP production increased rapidly within 1 min of light irradiation and subsequently decreased.

Li designed probe 28 (Fig. 21 a) [67] by encapsulating the perylene probe N,N′-bis(6-caproic acid)-3,4:9,10-perylenediimide (PDI) within ZIF-8, which demonstrated significant excimer emission from the perylene probe. The presence of ATP specifically triggered the decomposition of this nanocomposite, leading to the release of PDI probes. And the strong monomer emission reached the maximum after 40 min (Fig. 21b).

Similarly, Xinghua et al. introduced a more sensitive nanoprobe, probe 29 (Fig. 21c) [69], which incorporates carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) within a ZIF-8 framework. Upon the breakdown of the framework due to ATP, the fluorescence emission of CDs at 469 nm increased, while the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIE) of AuNCs at 660 nm decreased. Consequently, ATP levels can be sensitively measured using the intensity ratio of the fluorescence emissions at 469 nm–660 nm (I469/I660), with a LOD of 0.061 μM.

Feng et al. developed a dual-signal platform, probe 30 (Fig. 22 a) [70]. This innovative ATP sensor consists primarily of a molecular beacon (MB) encapsulated within ZIF-90, offering both fluorescent and homogeneous electrochemical signals with rapid response time (5 min) (Fig. 22b). Furthermore, probe 33 achieved ultra-sensitive detection with 0.38 nM as LOD using the fluorescence technique and 0.027 nM by the homogeneous electrochemical method.

Peng et al. reported a metal-based ZIF-90 complex, probe 31 (Fig. 22c) [71], created by encapsulating copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) in the presence of aluminum ions (Al3+) into ZIF-90 using a simple one-pot method. In the absence of ATP, probe 31 exhibited the characteristic fluorescence of CuNCs at an emission wavelength of 620 nm. Upon the addition of ATP, cleavage occurred, leading to a decrease in CuNC fluorescence and the emergence of a new fluorescence emission at 450 nm, independently under excitation at 360 nm, attributed to the release of the 2-ICA ligand from the probe. Probe 31 also realized a quick reaction to ATP, showing 1.5 min as the optimum reaction time. Since probe 31 displays dual-color fluorescence properties, it has proven effective in imaging the distribution of ATP in biofilms.

Chunyan's group introduced a series of ZIF-90-based nanopores with different features. Probe 32 (Fig. 23 a) [72], which encapsulates a rhodamine-based near-infrared (NIR) dye within ZIF-90, is used to monitor ATP levels in colorectal cancer cells (HCT116) and in a mouse model of colitis. Probe 33 (Fig. 23b) [73], which incorporates the NIR-emitting peroxynitrite (ONOO−)-sensitive unit CPQ, enables sequential detection of ATP at 740 nm and subsequent ONOO− detection by oxidative cleavage, resulting in green fluorescence emission at 510 nm. Additionally, probe 34 (Fig. 23c) [74], designed to harbor near-infrared fluorescent RhI within ZIF-90, also encapsulates the anticancer drug doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) and glucose oxidase (GOD). Given the significantly higher ATP levels in tumor cells compared to healthy cells, probe 34 demonstrates outstanding responsiveness to ATP while simultaneously eradicating cancer cells through chemotherapy via the release of DOX and GOD, which depletes glucose and starves the cancer cells.

Probes 35 and 36 [75] loaded a modified high fluorescence quantum yield dye, SiRhBSO2 (Fig. 24 a) and ORhBSO2 (Fig. 24b), respectively, into the ZIF-90 framework. This specific structure suppresses the twisted intramolecular charge transfer process and significantly enhances the quantum yields of rhodamine, resulting in high sensitivity with LODs of 7.56 μM and 6.6 μM, respectively. Notably, these two ZIF-based composites demonstrated strong performance in mitochondrial imaging and in diagnosing the acute liver injury, utilizing an excitation filter of 620 nm and an emission filter of 650 nm (Fig. 24c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent ATP probes based on multi-response channels
ce:section.ce:para: There has been a growing interest in developing various fluorescent probes capable of simultaneously detecting two or more analytes, which can provide critical information in health and disease states [76,77]. Here, dual-responsive fluorescent probes detecting ATP with oxidative species, sulfide, polarity, glutathione, and metal ions are recorded.

Oxidative stress is frequently cited as a cause of ATP disruption, which can lead to a variety of diseases [78]. In a recent study, Bo and colleagues developed a novel fluorescent probe, designated as compound 37 [79], by combining a rhodamine lactam scaffold with a caffeic acid moiety. This probe serves as a dual-color molecular platform, enabling simultaneous visualization of superoxide (O2 •−) at 470 nm and ATP at 588 nm (Fig. 25 a). Notably, probe 37 possesses dual-reversible properties, allowing for real-time monitoring of O2 •− (Fig. 25b) and ATP levels (Fig. 25c). The study demonstrated the efficacy of probe 37 in tracing the dynamics of O2 •− and ATP during hepatic ischemia-reperfusion injury (HIRI) in both hepatocytes (Fig. 25d) and mouse livers (Fig. 25e), thereby establishing a basis for future HIRI monitoring [80,81].

Peroxynitrite (ONOO−) is a reactive nitrogen species known to inhibit ATP production by oxidatively deactivating mitochondrial ATP synthase [82]. The sequential detection of ATP and ONOO− using probe 33 is illustrated in Fig. 23b [73]. Similarly, probe 38, developed by Jonathan's group, enables the simultaneous detection of ATP and ONOO− (Fig. 26 a) [83]. The boronate pinacol ester of the probe can be selectively oxidized by ONOO−, yielding the fluorescent product NA-OH (4-hydroxy-1,8-naphthalimide) with emission in the 500–575 nm range. Meanwhile, the rhodamine lactam/diethylenetriamine assembly binds ATP and emits fluorescence at 575–650 nm, providing a highly emissive output. Probe 38 was applied to monitor ATP in HL-7702 treated with SIN-1 or SIN-1/uric acid (Fig. 26b) and omy A or omy A/ATP (Fig. 26c), promising to depict the dynamics of ATP and ONOO− during APAP-induced hepatotoxicity.

H2O2, as the typical ROS model [84], was targeted as well. Li's group developed probe 39, which was utilized for simultaneous and dynamic detection of H2O2 and ATP through dual-modal imaging. Thin zirconium-based MOF encapsulated AuNR as the main frame, followed by modification of the ROS-responsive molecules 2-mercaptohydroquinone (2-MHQ) and 6-carboxyfluorescein-labeled ATP aptamer (FAM-Apt) [84] (Fig. 27 a). By integrating Dual-Modal imaging, ATP and ROS could be identified by confocal fluorescence microscopy and Raman imaging microscopy, respectively and independently (Fig. 27b).

Tian's team designed a novel single two-photon fluorescence-lifetime-based probe, probe 40, with three moieties included (Fig. 28 a) [85]. One is the H2O2-responsive moiety, of which the activity of naphthalimide fluorophore can be activated by the addition of H2O2. The ATP-responsive moiety is a rhodamine complex modified with a diethylenetriamine structure. The addition of ATP opens the spirolactam ring in this moiety and then leads to significant fluorescence. The intermediate pyridinium cation is responsible for targeting mitochondria due to highly negative mitochondrial membrane potential. By incorporating these three moieties, probe 40 can achieve real-time imaging and simultaneous quantification of mitochondrial H2O2 and ATP in neurons (Fig. 28b and c).

Abnormal sulfide expression is associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and is considered a marker for potential brain disorders and cancer [86–88]. Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) plays a role in various physiological functions, such as vasodilation, inflammation regulation, and cell protection [89]. Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is involved in the regulation of blood vessels, the heart, the lungs, and the nervous system, having a significant impact on the cardiovascular system under normal conditions [90]. Below, we discuss the methods for measuring and SO2 in conjunction with ATP sensing.

Probe 41 [91], developed by Baodui's group, enables simultaneous detection of ATP at a near-infrared fluorescence of 577 nm and hydrogen sulfide (H2S) at a green fluorescence of 528 nm (Fig. 29 a). The probe also allows for in vivo brain imaging of mice following tail vein injection. By using probe 37, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of H2S and ATP was higher in wild-type mice compared to Alzheimer's disease (AD) model mice (Fig. 29b).

As shown in Fig. 29c, Yin's team generated a dual-site sensing platform for bioimaging ATP and H2S simultaneously, probe 42 [92]. The reaction between probe 42 and ATP is ascribed into the hydrogen bond and π-π stacking interaction, leading to red emissions. Meanwhile, a highly selective naphthalene azide group was applied to target H2S and emit green signals. The relationship between ATP and H2S was explored by probe 42 in HCT116 and HeLa cells, providing a reference for cancer research.

In the development of probe 43 (Fig. 30 a) [93], Qian Yong et al. utilized both an aggregation strategy for ATP sensing and a disaggregation strategy for H2S sensing. The probe consists of four components. The orange segment is a vinyl pyridine-substituted tetraphenylethylene that serves as an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) photosensitizer with red emission. The green portion includes an amide and a positively charged pyridinium ring that act as ATP binding sites through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. The red portion features a disulfide bond that reacts with H2S, leading to structural breakdown and fluorescence quenching. Additionally, the polyethylene glycol (PEG) tail with n = 160, 350, and 750, respectively, enhances the probe's biocompatibility and solubility in aqueous solutions. Especially, one type of Probe 43 with n = 750 possessed good ability in ROS generation under short white light irritation (3 min) (Fig. 30b), resulting in half of the HepG-2 cells killed and almost all of them died after 5 min (Fig. 30c), indicating as a superior photosensitizer for PDT of cancer cells.

Probe 44, designed by the same research group, features a structure with ATP and H2S reaction sites, a unique TPEPy unit, and a long alkyl chain tail (Fig. 30d). [94]. The TPEPy unit makes probe 44 a red AIE-based emission. It is noteworthy that probe 44 exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward ATP and H2S in SMMC cells under physiological conditions (Fig. 30e).

Probe 45 was envisioned for tracking lysosomal ATP and H2S during liver injury (Fig. 31 ) [95]. As a dual-site fluorescent sensor, probe 45 assembled diethylenetriamine and azide (–N3) to track ATP and H2S, respectively. In the acidic lysosomal environment, the spirocyclic lactam of probe 45 was turned on by binding with ATP and its yellow fluorescence at 560 nm by rhodamine 6G (Rh6G). And the presence of H2S will activate the 1,8-naphthalimide hybrid fluorophores to light at 530 nm. Finally, probe 45 successfully enables the fluorescence of HepG2 cells under various treatment conditions and in vivo imaging of zebrafish.

Chunyan and her team fabricated a sequential dual-channel fluorescent nanoprobe, probe 46 (Fig. 32 a) [96]. This nanoprobe interacts with ATP, releasing the SO2-sensitive dye (CPH) from the ZIF-90 framework and increasing near-infrared fluorescence at 685 nm. Subsequently, SO2 reacts with the released CPH, which enhances fluorescence at 580 nm (Fig. 32b).

Probe 47 [97] (Fig. 33 a) is the first sensor developed for simultaneously monitoring lysosomal polarity and ATP using different emissions. In this system, morpholine functions as the lysosomal targeting group. Diethylenetriamine captures ATP, rapidly activating the rhodamine for strong red emission. Furthermore, the green emission initially increases and then decreases as polarity increases due to the intra-molecular charge transfer (ICT) effect. This effect shows promise in detecting the dynamics of ATP and polarity under various stress conditions, including H2O2-induced autophagy in the zebrafish model (Fig. 33b).

Glutathione (GSH) plays a critical role in removing the reactive species, therefore protecting cells from oxidative damage and maintaining redox homeostasis [98]. Li's team reported a novel nanodevice, probe 48, can be activated sequentially by the simultaneous existence of GSH and ATP (Fig. 34 a) [99]. Probe 48 is comprised of two parts. One is hybridized DNA strands, including ATP aptamer-based A-strand, and modified disulfide bond-incorporated G-strand. The other part is TPP conjugated NP. The presence of both GSH and ATP dissociated the hybridization of strands, thereby relieving the affinity between the quencher and Cy3 from two strands, resulting in the activation of the fluorescent signal. Additionally, with the assistance of triphenylphosphonium, probe 48 could be specifically targeted to mitochondria. Notably, probe 48 demonstrates the ability to detect tumors in an MCF-7 tumor-bearing mice model. And the signal is hard to see in control groups (A-nG/NT and mA-G/NT) and drug-treated groups (3-BP) (Fig. 34b).
Copper ions (Cu2+) play a critical role in biological systems, supporting cellular respiration, energy metabolism, removal of reactive oxygen species, and serving as cofactors for enzymes [100–102].

Shuhan's group developed probe 49 (Fig. 35 a) [103] and probe 50 (Fig. 35b) [104] to sequentially recognize Cu2+ and ATP. Probe 49 features one triphenylamine thiophene and two 1,5,9-triazacyclododecyl units. Upon Cu2+ binding, the coordination between the 1,5,9-triazacyclododecyl units of probe 49 and Cu2+ quenches the fluorescence. The addition of ATP is recognized by the thiazolyl group through π–π interactions and electronic attraction, resulting in a significant increase in green fluorescence. Additionally, the benzothiazole group allows the probe to target mitochondria.

Similarly, probe 50(Fig. 35b)[104] also contains a 1,5,9-triazacyclododecyl unit for detecting Cu2+. The coordination of ATP releases or weakens the bonding of the 1,5,9-triazacyclododecane group to Cu2+, restoring the fluorescence of the probe. This “on-off-on” fluorescence capability enables the probe to measure Cu2+ and ATP concentrations in mitochondria within cells and zebrafish.

Zinc is the second most abundant trace element in the body and is essential for cell signaling, proliferation, and differentiation, acting as either a structural component or a regulatory factor [105,106]. Cadmium, due to its similar properties and comparable spectral changes, presents a significant interference in zinc detection [107].

Gaolei's group synthesized probe 51 (Fig. 36 ) [107], which is based on pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde and 2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethanol. This probe exhibited a gradual and remarkable fluorescent enhancement at 456 nm with sequential supplementation of Zn2+ and ATP, demonstrating an “off-medium-strong” response.

Probe 52 (Fig. 37 a) [108] is a novel fluorescent sensor for the sequential ratiometric detection of Zn2+ and ATP. The probe contains an 8-aminoquinoline unit, which serves as both fluorophore and binding receptor for Zn2+. The formed probe 52-Zn2+ complexes exhibit high selectivity and sensitivity toward ATP, with a detection limit of 11.1 nM. Additionally, the aforementioned probe 5 (Fig. 4a) [36], with a “red-green-red” strategy, demonstrated sequential identification of Zn2+ and ATP.

Probe 53 (Fig. 37b) [109] is a specific dual-responsive conjugate designed for the simultaneous measurement of ATP and Zn2+. Upon ATP addition, the sequence aptamer is deconstructed, leading to the subsequent recovery of Cy5 emission, with a detection limit for ATP of 41.2 μM. Meanwhile, the 5′ thiol of the sequence aptamer is conjugated with a chemosensor, consisting of fluorescein as a fluorophore and (2-aminoethyl)bis(2-pyridylmethyl)amine for Zn2+ recognition. The coordination of Zn2+ to the probe 53 results in the recovery of fluorescence. Notably, the introduction of a cholesterol group allows the probe to anchor into the plasma membrane of cells (Fig. 37c).

A recent study introduced a novel approach involving probe 54 [110], which utilizes sulfur vacancies in MoS2 nanosheets (NSs) combined with distinct thiolate DNA molecules to detect K+ and ATP. This flare, composed of a donor and acceptor, is attached to the single-stranded DNA (ssDNA), generating two distinct emission peaks at 584 and 525 nm (Fig. 38 a). On one hand, the binding of G4-thiolate ssDNA duplexes to NSs enables recognition and ratiometric sensing of K+ (Fig. 38b). On the other hand, Apt-thiolate ssDNA duplexes allow the recognition of ATP based on the NSs (Fig. 38b). The introduction of exogenous molecules frees the flares and triggers a FRET process, resulting in significant changes in the I584/I525 value (Fig. 38c and d), which offers potential diagnostic applications in various clinical settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent ATP probes based on applications

ce:section.ce:para: The specific targeting of probes to mitochondria relies on ATP accumulation [68]. Besides, the specific elements are beneficial to localization. TPP (in probes 6 and 48), as a lipophilic cationic species, can traverse mitochondrial membranes and accumulate in the negatively charged mitochondria [111,112], therefore widely applied to mitochondria-targeting. The benzothiazole group (in probe 49) also can provide the mitochondria targeting ability [113].

Xiao-bing and colleagues presented probe 55 (Fig. 39 a) [114], specifically choosing Rh6G as the donor fluorophore due to its precise localization in the mitochondrion [115,116]. Additionally, the two-photon-excited fluorophore with far-red emission was chosen as the signal reporter. Alkylamines were assembled towards ATP. Importantly, probe 55 was shown to have a very rapid response to ATP (5 mM), achieving the maximum fluorescence intensity within 200s. In addition, probe 55 was proved to determine the dynamic changes in mitochondrial ATP levels under alterations of glucose concentration (Fig. 39b and c).

In addition to mitochondria, lysosomes also play a critical role in ATP storage and are key regulators of apoptosis and autophagy in both health and disease [117–119]. The acidic environment of lysosomes (pH 4.5–5.5) can present challenges for probe stability. In probe 3 and 47, morpholine serves as the lysosomal targeting group due to its effective lysosome-targeting properties.

Zhaohui et al. developed a real-time lysosomal-specific fluorescent CD probe 56 [120], synthesized in one step under microwave conditions using citric acid, triethylenetetramine, and Rh6G (Fig. 40 a). The yellow fluorescence of the CDs is only triggered under the presence of both ATP and specific pH levels when entering the acidic lysosomal environment. Furthermore, about two years later, Zhaohui's group designed a novel assembly probe 57 [121] (Fig. 40b) by integrating probe 56 with upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs). These UCNPs absorb lower-energy near-infrared photons and emit higher-energy photons, enhancing their complementary advantages. Notably, probe 57 was utilized in exploring the production and remediation pathways of acetaminophen (APAP)-induced hepatotoxicity in mice models for the first time (Fig. 40cd).

Jiadong and colleagues [122] designed a self-assembled DNA triangular prism nanomachine probe 58 capable of responding to the acidic environment and ATP (Fig. 41 a). Only in the presence of both ATP and the specific acidic environment of lysosomes, the appropriate structural alteration brings the fluorescent dyes into close proximity, facilitating FRET signals to be recorded within the emission range of 646–730 nm (Fig. 41b).

Probe 59 [123], developed by Hongping's group, uses Mn(II) as the central metal element (Fig. 42 a). This incorporation not only extends the probe's fluorescence lifetime, enabling advanced fluorescence lifetime imaging, but also allows for association with anionic ATP via electrostatic interactions due to its positive charge. The ethoxy group in the probe contributes to its exceptional NIR nonlinear fluorescent properties. By combining with multiphoton fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (MP-FLIM), it is further demonstrated that probe 59 displays lysosome-specific targeting behavior, which can monitor lysosome-related ATP migration under NIR laser light. (Fig. 42b).

It's worth noting that nuclear ATP plays a crucial role in solubilizing chromatin and nuclear macromolecules [124,125]. Kyo Han Ahn and colleagues presented a small-molecule probe 60 (Fig. 43 ) [126], which demonstrated a strong overlap with the nucleus tracker, Nuclear-ID, as the first small molecule that fluorescently senses nuclear ATP. In the presence of ATP, the newly formed ATP-bound Zn(II) complex replaced the alkoxide-Zn(II) complex, resulting in strong fluorescence emission from the benzocoumarin core. Notably, probe 60 effectively distinguished between cancerous and normal cells and tissues, indicating that nuclear ATP levels were higher in the cancerous group compared to the normal group.

Since ATP cannot be synthesized outside cells, extracellular ATP levels are strictly regulated by the balance between its release and degradation [127]. These extracellular levels reflect the in vivo state, underscoring the importance of establishing reliable, efficient, and sensitive methods for detecting intracellular, mitochondrial, and extracellular ATP molecules.

Chemically synthesized probes incorporating natural membrane components have been shown to target cell membranes [128]. In probe 53, the cholesterol group was accepted to anchor the probe in the plasma membrane of cells.

Fan and colleagues developed a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent probe, probe 61 (Fig. 44 a) [129], for the detection of extracellular ATP. This probe leverages electrostatic and π–π interactions between the phosphates of ATP and the macrocyclic polyamines, adenines, and benzene rings of the probe, achieving a high sensitivity with a limit of detection of 21 nM. Significantly, due to its similarity to and compatibility with cell membranes, probe 61 can specifically target cell membranes, as confirmed by an overlap coefficient of up to 0.95 with the commercial cell membrane dye Dio (Fig. 44b).

Liquid biopsy is a promising approach for the early diagnosis, medication monitoring, and prognosis assessment of solid tumors due to its speed, minimal invasiveness, and patient compliance [130]. Given ATP's essential roles in disease and as a key phosphate-containing metabolite in blood serum, establishing rapid and sensitive assays for liquid biopsy detection is vital.

Jiannian and colleagues reported probe 62 (Fig. 45 ) [131], an assembly comprising cobalt(II)-based layered double hydroxide (LDH) coupled with FAM-labeled DNA through electrostatic and ligand exchange interactions. The quenched FAM-labeled DNA detaches due to the stronger affinity of ATP to LDH, releasing a signal. Notably, the probe exhibits an excellent response to 50-fold diluted human serum.

Probe 63 (Fig. 46 ) [132], developed by Chuan's group, is a novel dual-signal probe with four components: ATP-Apt for ATP capture, berberine as the signal reporter, salt as the aggregation inducer, and MoS2 nanosheet as the background eliminator and fluorescence anisotropy signal amplifier. The schematic illustration shows that the presence of ATP leads to the detachment of the ATP-aptamer/ATP/berberine complex from the surface of the MoS2 nanosheet. Consequently, the fluorescence signal switches from “OFF” to “ON,” while the fluorescence anisotropy signal changes from “ON” to “OFF.” This dual-signal evaluation method is applied to ATP detection and exhibits excellent response to 50-fold diluted human serum. Surprisingly, the detection limit of fluorescence signal and fluorescence anisotropy can reach extremely low, calculated to be 40.0 nM and 20.8 nM, respectively.

Yao's group developed a detection probe, probe 64 (Fig. 47 a) [132], composed of titanium carbide (TC) nanosheets modified with a ROX-tagged ATP-aptamer. This probe has high biocompatibility and is capable of accurately detecting ATP in living cells, body fluids such as mouse serum, mouse urine, and human serum (Fig. 47b), as well as in mouse tumor models (Fig. 47c).

Microneedles are an array of miniaturized needles that offer unique advantages for in situ detection, including micrometer-scale dimensions, minimal invasiveness, ease of operation, and the ability to combine with other detection technologies [133–135]. Li et al. developed probe 65 [136], which consists of methacrylated hyaluronic acid (HA) hydrogel microneedles as a platform, as well as rhodamine dye (RhE) conjugated to gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) for ATP detection and display. In their resting state, AuNCs emit green fluorescence. The presence of ATP induces efficient FRET between AuNCs and RhE, leading to decreased green fluorescence of AuNCs and increased red fluorescence of RhE (Fig. 48 a). The fabricated probe 65 was successfully applied to agarose hydrogel loaded with varying concentrations of ATP (Fig. 48b).

Cancer, as a kind of malignant tumor, is the world's second leading cause of human death, and the mechanisms remain elusive [137]. On the one hand, real-time monitoring of ATP dynamics is crucial to elucidate ATP's role in cancer development. On the other hand, ATP would be a highly promising target for probes to assist cancer diagnosis.

In probe 66 [138] (Fig. 49 a), biotin was introduced as a tumor-targeted group because it can be preferentially absorbed into cancer cells, combined with si-rhodamine as a fluorophore and diethylenetriamine as the ATP recognition group. In the absence of ATP, probe 66 displayed very low fluorescence, while upon the addition of ATP, it produced a strong red fluorescence signal at 675 nm. Next, the tumor-bearing mice were utilized for fluorescence imaging by probe 66 at different times. Compared to SiR as a control group, the tumor-targeting ability of probe 66 is verified (Fig. 49b). Attractively, a precise fluorescent signal is only seen in the tumor-affected domain with probe 66 compared to that with SiR (Fig. 49c).

Probe 67 is a self-assembled ATP probe designed in Yoon's group for melanoma cell imaging [24]. The fluorescent core terpyridine-Zn2+ complex (Fig. 50 a) became invisible due to the formation of nanoassemblies in aqueous solutions (Fig. 50b). When the presence of ATP, the newly formed complex between positively charged terpyridine-Zn2+ group and negatively charged ATP “turn-on” the fluorescence emission. As shown in Fig. 50c, the fluorescence intensity was dependent on the concentration of probe 67, and the bright fluorescence images of melanoma cells were obtained by incubating with probe 67 for 30 min at 37 °C.

Probe 68 and Probe 69 utilize similar strategies for detecting and real-time imaging ATP. Through a one-pot self-assembly method, Zhouping used ZIF-90 as the base carrier, with NIR dyes CYA and CYQ serving as stable fluorescent signals, respectively (Fig. 51 a) [139]. Notably, Probe 68 achieved NIR spectroscopic detection of ATP within 2 min with a detection limit as low as 0.13 mM and also performed well in real-time ATP detection in viable bacterial cells (Fig. 51b), achieving a limit of detection as low as 74 CFU ml−1.

ATP is the primary molecule responsible for transferring and storing energy in living cells and is produced in mitochondria. Recent advancements in fluorescent probes have enabled the visualization and even precise measurement of ATP levels in intra- and extracellular environments, as well as in complex biological processes, on a nanoscale level. In this review, we discuss advancements in fluorescent ATP probes for use in living cells and biological samples. These probes are classified according to fluorophores, sensing mechanisms, multi-response channels, and cellular localization. Most fluorescent ATP probes consist of organic fluorophores and recognition sites. The interactions between fluorescent probes and ATP involve electrostatic, hydrogen bond, and π-π stacking interactions, and combinations thereof. Organelle-targeted fluorescent probes can be synthesized through specific modifications. Moreover, the probes have been applied to various conditions, including cell lines, microbes, biofilms, and human biofluids. Gradually, promising research has demonstrated that ATP measurement can help enhance biosensing concentrations of fibroblast activation protein (FAP) in vivo [141], distinguish normal cells from cancer cells [66], monitor hepatic ischemia−reperfusion injury (HIRI) [72,73], and shed light on pathogenesis and allow for precise evaluation of drug-induced hepatotoxicity [105]. We have now included Table 1 , which provides details on all the probes. Specifically, we summarize their targets, selectivity, sensitivity (including the limit of detection and linear range), and applications. Due to the diverse and complex detection environments associated with each probe, defining an exact analysis time remains elusive. It is anticipated that ratiometric ATP imaging will be increasingly employed in medical diagnostics and evaluations, providing detailed nanoscale insights and paving the way for advances in personalized therapies in the future.

Despite advancements, the development of fluorescence sensing probes for ATP remains a significant challenge, and corresponding solutions have been proposed. First, structurally similar molecules such as AMP and ADP complicate selectivity within biological systems. To overcome this, researchers must devise novel strategies to target ATP precisely based on structure or characteristics. Second, most fluorescent ATP probes utilize the visible light spectrum for excitation and emission, limiting their effectiveness in imaging ATP in living organisms. NIR fluorescent probes, however, offer superior in vivo imaging due to their enhanced tissue penetration capabilities. Specifically, NIR-II fluorescent probes provide deeper tissue penetration with reduced photon scattering and autofluorescence. This has led to expectations for the future development of NIR-II fluorescent ATP probes with targeted organelle specificity. Third, AIE molecules, or AIEgen, are non-emissive in solution due to free-rotation-induced non-radiative decay. In contrast, AIEgen emits light when aggregated, as the restrictive intramolecular motions are reinstated. Exploring new ATP sensing approaches based on AIEgen is crucial, particularly for organelle-targeted fluorescent ATP probes, which have significant potential in clinical applications.
